gua: Hexagram 1 is like the sky in ceaseless motion: it points to strong creative energy and the will to act. You may be able to move things forward, but the greater your power, the more important it is to know when to prepare and when to move. Lasting success depends on discipline and a willingness to learn from experienced people, not drive alone. At the peak, watch especially for pride and overreach.
lines:
In short: Your ability is still below the surface; prepare instead of rushing to prove yourself.
What it means: A hidden dragon is not powerless. Its surroundings and experience are simply not ready for it to emerge.
Try: Build core skills, gather information, and wait for a genuine opening.
Avoid: Making premature promises or forcing your way forward just to be noticed.

In short: Your ability is becoming visible; this is a good time to meet experienced allies or mentors.
What it means: The dragon entering the fields marks a shift from private preparation to useful work in the world.
Try: Show real results, start conversations, and welcome informed guidance.
Avoid: Focusing so much on displaying your talent that you miss a chance to cooperate.

In short: Events are accelerating, so work hard while continuing to check the risks.
What it means: This in-between position brings more work and responsibility, while small lapses can have larger consequences.
Try: Keep a sustainable pace, verify important details, and leave room to correct decisions.
Avoid: Mistaking busyness for progress or letting early success lower your guard.

In short: You are at the edge of a leap; test the opportunity without staking everything on it.
What it means: The dragon may rise from the depths or remain there a little longer. The important point is that the choice is still open.
Try: Run a small experiment and keep a workable way back.
Avoid: Taking a leap beyond your capacity because other people expect it.

In short: You can now exert real influence; use that position to help more than yourself.
What it means: A dragon flying in the sky suggests that talent, authority, and timing are working together.
Try: Set a clear direction and work with people mature enough to share a major responsibility.
Avoid: Claiming all the credit or using status to silence disagreement.

In short: Rising too high without knowing when to ease back can turn an advantage into regret.
What it means: The dragon has climbed until there is nowhere left to go, an image of overexpansion and pride.
Try: Slow down voluntarily, share authority, and review the real cost of continuing.
Avoid: Doubling down on a choice with no room left simply to save face.

gua: Hexagram 2 is like the earth carrying and nourishing everything upon it. Its strength lies in receiving, supporting, and turning direction into practical reality. This is not passive obedience: first understand where you are going, then patiently create the conditions in which the work can take root. Flexibility with clear principles can travel farther than force.
lines:
In short: A very small sign may reveal a problem that will grow later.
What it means: Feeling frost underfoot tells you winter is coming; early warnings deserve attention.
Try: Investigate the cause and adjust habits or plans while the cost is still low.
Avoid: Repeating “it is probably fine” until the problem has hardened.

In short: Doing your part honestly and steadily can be enough to produce results.
What it means: The earth is straight, square, and broad—reliable, clear, and able to make room for others.
Try: Build sound standards and processes, then earn trust through consistency.
Avoid: Losing your boundaries to please others or drawing attention to every contribution.

In short: You do not need to compete for credit; first help bring the shared work to completion.
What it means: Keeping talent contained is not self-erasure. It means letting the result speak at the right time.
Try: Support the common goal, honor your commitments, and keep a clear record of your work.
Avoid: Withdrawing because praise is delayed or quietly fighting for the spotlight.

In short: In a sensitive situation, restraint in speech and action can prevent needless trouble.
What it means: Tying a sack closed suggests keeping information, emotion, and allegiance from spilling out carelessly.
Try: Respect confidentiality, observe closely, and handle what is actually under your control.
Avoid: Joining gossip or taking sides before the facts are clear.

In short: Quiet substance earns more durable trust than a dazzling display.
What it means: A yellow lower garment suggests a centered, unshowy way of carrying responsibility.
Try: Build influence through fairness, reliability, and care for the whole situation.
Avoid: Confusing humility with having no position, or using polish to hide weak substance.

In short: When two forces remain locked in combat, both sides may be badly hurt.
What it means: Dragons fighting in the open shows cooperation turning into a struggle for control.
Try: Stop the escalation and renegotiate roles and limits before losses spread.
Avoid: Consuming the relationship and its resources merely to prove who is right.

gua: Hexagram 3 describes the confusion at the birth of something new. A direction exists, but the people, processes, and conditions are not yet aligned. Like a shoot pushing through compacted soil, the resistance is real, but so is the life within it. Build order, find help, and divide the large aim into manageable beginnings.
lines:
In short: When the beginning is blocked, steady yourself instead of changing direction in panic.
What it means: Hesitation need not mean failure; the entrance is still unclear and needs a dependable foothold.
Try: Confirm the goal, find trustworthy allies, and take the smallest workable step.
Avoid: Expanding impulsively or tearing everything down at the first obstacle.

In short: Someone approaching may be a potential partner, not a threat, but trust needs time.
What it means: The circling riders and delayed marriage suggest a relationship whose intentions are not yet clear.
Try: Learn what the other party wants and build confidence gradually.
Avoid: Making a hurried commitment because of loneliness or pressure.

In short: Chasing into an unfamiliar forest without a guide is how people get lost.
What it means: Seeing the deer is not enough; you may have spotted an opportunity without having the information or support to pursue it.
Try: Research further, consult someone who knows the terrain, or let this round go.
Avoid: Continuing only because you have already invested something.

In short: Do not remain trapped in waiting; a direct invitation may open the way.
What it means: The circling rider who finally proposes suggests mutual interest lacking a clear request.
Try: State what you need and what you can contribute.
Avoid: Preserving ambiguity because you are afraid of rejection.

In short: Resources are not yet reaching the people who need them; handle small improvements before attempting sweeping reform.
What it means: Good intentions are obstructed by limited authority, broken communication, or an immature system.
Try: Complete local fixes and gradually connect the necessary people and processes.
Avoid: Announcing a total solution while the foundation remains unstable.

In short: Endless indecision will drain both emotion and resources.
What it means: Circling on horseback until tears flow shows that delay is no longer producing useful information.
Try: Set a deadline, then choose either to proceed or to leave.
Avoid: Replaying the same question without changing any of its conditions.

gua: Hexagram 4 concerns inexperience, learning, and the need for guidance. Not knowing is no disgrace; pretending to know, or repeatedly asking without practicing, is the greater obstacle. Find a trustworthy teacher and develop judgment through concrete exercises. A good teacher supplies method and boundaries rather than living the learner's life for them.
lines:
In short: Beginners need structure, but structure should help them move, not bind them forever.
What it means: Removing restraints before walking suggests that discipline should correct behavior, not humiliate or control a person.
Try: Set basic rules and practice the right response immediately after a mistake.
Avoid: Relying on punishment until fear replaces thought.

In short: Patiently making room for a beginner is a practical form of leadership.
What it means: Teaching the inexperienced and managing a household both require care, responsibility, and usable methods.
Try: Teach in stages, give feedback, and let the learner assume responsibility gradually.
Avoid: Turning patience into permissiveness or doing everything for them.

In short: If you are captivated only by status or wealth, you may lose your own judgment.
What it means: The woman who abandons herself at the sight of a rich man is an image of being blinded by prestige, resources, or appearance.
Try: Name the qualities that truly matter to you and keep your own boundaries.
Avoid: Handing over your judgment because of admiration or advantage.

In short: Remaining trapped in ignorance while refusing help makes escape increasingly difficult.
What it means: This is more than temporary confusion; a closed perspective has become a prison.
Try: Admit where you are stuck and change the teacher, method, or learning environment.
Avoid: Pretending to understand because you are embarrassed.

In short: Curiosity and willingness to learn are a beginner's greatest strengths.
What it means: Childlike inexperience has not yet hardened into conceit, so sound guidance can still enter easily.
Try: Ask candid questions, follow the method, and record your progress.
Avoid: Skipping foundations in order to appear clever.

In short: Correction may be firm, but its purpose should be preventing harm, not attacking a person.
What it means: Striking ignorance as revenge creates resistance; building a defense has educational value.
Try: Stop harmful conduct, explain the consequences, and offer a path to improvement.
Avoid: Public humiliation, permanent labels, or answering harm with more harm.

gua: Hexagram 5 is active, prepared waiting rather than inactivity. Like waiting for the river to become passable, you can still gather resources, confirm the route, and care for your health. Anxiety often pushes people to move too soon, but effort cannot compensate for missing conditions. Preparation is what lets you use the opportunity when it arrives.
lines:
In short: The danger is still distant; prepare at the edge instead of walking toward trouble.
What it means: Waiting in the outskirts means you retain a safe distance and can preserve an ordinary rhythm.
Try: Ready your information, funds, and backup plans.
Avoid: Taking an early risk simply because waiting feels uncomfortable.

In short: Minor friction and gossip may arise, but patience can let the disturbance pass.
What it means: Waiting on the sand places you near the water without yet being trapped; the problem remains manageable.
Try: Answer facts plainly and let time test the claims.
Avoid: Turning every provocation into a larger confrontation.

In short: You are close to the danger zone; staying there too long invites loss.
What it means: Mud hinders movement and leaves you exposed to outside trouble.
Try: Reduce exposure, prepare an exit, and repair vulnerabilities quickly.
Avoid: Remaining in a worsening environment on the strength of wishful thinking.

In short: The situation is already causing real harm; first leave the center of danger.
What it means: Waiting in blood is not a call for greater endurance. It says the cost has become too high.
Try: Seek help, stop the loss, and protect safety and essential resources.
Avoid: Staying in a harmful place merely to preserve pride.

In short: You may still nourish yourself and live well while waiting.
What it means: Food and drink represent replenishment; composure means not allowing anxiety to consume you.
Try: Care for your body, sustain relationships, and keep a pace you can maintain.
Avoid: Using indulgence to escape the issue or treating rest as a failure.

In short: Unexpected visitors may carry a way out; hear them before deciding.
What it means: Three uninvited guests arriving at the pit suggest that help may come from an unforeseen source.
Try: Suspend assumptions and learn what they can actually offer.
Avoid: Rejecting every form of help because the situation feels out of control.

gua: Hexagram 6 deals with conflict, dispute, and failing trust. You may have a valid case, but pursuing the fight to its absolute end can cost more than the original problem. Clarify the facts, responsibilities, and the outcome you actually want before choosing negotiation, mediation, or withdrawal. When needed, involve a fair and qualified third party rather than relying on anger to decide the winner.
lines:
In short: When a dispute is young, keep your claims measured and bring it to an early close.
What it means: A small quarrel prolonged through repeated words can turn into a battle over identity and position.
Try: Preserve evidence, clarify briefly, and propose a workable settlement.
Avoid: Answering every attack in public.

In short: A direct fight is unlikely to succeed; withdraw to safer ground.
What it means: Returning home from an unwinnable case is about protecting people and the basic foundation.
Try: Stop widening the conflict and seek settlement or professional advice.
Avoid: Pulling more people into the struggle because you cannot bear to yield.

In short: Rely on the character and skill you have built over time instead of fighting to prove your worth.
What it means: Living on established virtue means preserving credibility; serving a powerful cause does not require taking its glory.
Try: Fulfill your duty and keep a record of your work.
Avoid: Competing for recognition where power is sharply unequal.

In short: You may decide that this fight is not worth allowing it to govern your life.
What it means: Being unable to continue the suit need not mean surrender; it can be a return to a healthier course.
Try: Accept a workable boundary and redirect energy to important goals.
Avoid: Calling it peace while continuing a private campaign of revenge.

In short: If a dispute must be decided, a fair process can produce a clear result.
What it means: Conditions now favor evidence and established rules rather than volume or influence.
Try: Organize the facts and approach a neutral person with real authority.
Avoid: Hiding inconvenient evidence or manipulating the procedure.

In short: Honor won through relentless conflict may be taken away just as quickly.
What it means: A ceremonial belt repeatedly granted and removed shows a victory with no stable foundation.
Try: Ask whether you want the problem solved or simply want to defeat the other person.
Avoid: Becoming addicted to winning and public humiliation.

gua: Hexagram 7 compares a group to an army: discipline, roles, and trustworthy command matter. In a major task or crisis, enthusiasm cannot replace clear instructions and logistical support. Leaders must bear consequences, while members need to understand their scope of responsibility. Mobilization driven by private resentment or ambition will quickly turn inward.
lines:
In short: Set the rules before action begins, or even a large group will descend into disorder.
What it means: An army puts discipline before deployment because a chaotic opening is difficult to repair later.
Try: Confirm the goal, roles, decision process, and conditions for stopping.
Avoid: Making people guess as they go or issuing contradictory orders.

In short: A leader must understand conditions on the ground to retain support.
What it means: Being present within the army shows leadership as shared responsibility, not distant command.
Try: Learn from the front line and distribute resources and recognition fairly.
Avoid: Appearing only for success and blaming subordinates for failure.

In short: Giving command to someone unfit for it can make the whole team pay.
What it means: A cart carrying corpses depicts confused leadership, with living people dragged down by bad decisions.
Try: Review competence and authority immediately, and replace the person if necessary.
Avoid: Preserving a failed arrangement out of sentiment.

In short: Withdrawing to the flank is not defeat when it prevents pointless loss.
What it means: Moving the army to safer ground is a deliberate adjustment to unfavorable conditions.
Try: Preserve strength, improve supplies and information, and wait for a better opening.
Avoid: Fighting a worthless battle merely to protect a slogan about morale.

In short: When the problem is clear, act—but put the right person in charge.
What it means: Game in the field can be taken, yet the contrast between the elder son commanding and the inexperienced carrying corpses warns about appointments.
Try: Give execution to a mature leader and have others follow the agreed plan.
Avoid: Multiple people competing to command or outsiders micromanaging the work.

In short: After success, distribute rewards carefully and do not hand lasting authority to unreliable people.
What it means: Founding states and granting offices is the work of governing after conflict.
Try: Reward contributions, but reserve key positions for proven character and ability.
Avoid: Converting a short-term achievement directly into long-term decision power.

gua: Hexagram 8 concerns alliance, closeness, and belonging. Good cooperation is not a matter of including everyone indiscriminately; it requires sincerity and compatible aims. You must also become someone others can reliably join. Waiting too long to declare your commitment may cause the moment for trust to pass.
lines:
In short: Trustworthiness is the starting point; substance can fill the relationship over time.
What it means: A plain earthen jar can hold water because it has no cracks, not because it looks impressive.
Try: Begin with small promises and keep words and actions aligned.
Avoid: Replacing reliability with polished language.

In short: Join only after you know that you genuinely agree from within.
What it means: Closeness arising internally has a stronger base than cooperation held together by external gain alone.
Try: Clarify values and expectations, then choose where you truly want to invest.
Avoid: Forcing yourself to belong because you fear being alone.

In short: The closer you bind yourself to unsuitable people, the more their conduct can harm you.
What it means: The character of your allies affects your direction, safety, and reputation.
Try: Check whether they respect boundaries and honor commitments.
Avoid: Explaining away conduct you already know is dangerous.

In short: Cooperation can be open and visible rather than maintained through private favors.
What it means: Outward alliance suggests a relationship that can withstand scrutiny and serve a broader interest.
Try: Make rules and terms transparent to everyone concerned.
Avoid: Building an inner circle through favors and suspicion.

In short: Mature leadership does not force everyone to follow; it leaves a genuine way out.
What it means: A hunt enclosed on only three sides lets the animals escape, so those who remain do so by choice.
Try: Invite clearly, respect autonomy, and earn loyalty through results.
Avoid: Using guilt, pressure, or blocked exits to manufacture commitment.

In short: A group without a recognized common direction will eventually pull apart.
What it means: Lacking a head does not merely mean lacking a strong person; it means lacking an accepted center and order.
Try: Establish the shared purpose and how coordination will work.
Avoid: Relying on affection alone until a crisis reveals that nobody is responsible.

gua: Hexagram 9 describes gradual accumulation and temporary restraint. Clouds have gathered but rain has not yet fallen: conditions are developing, though not enough for a major breakthrough. Refine details, save resources, and improve relationships so that small gains can build. Trying to reverse everything at once may damage what is already working.
lines:
In short: Correcting your course early is itself a positive step.
What it means: Returning to the road shows that an early mistake is inexpensive to repair; changing your mind need not be embarrassing.
Try: Admit the deviation and resume a sustainable approach immediately.
Avoid: Going farther in the wrong direction just to defend your first choice.

In short: When someone pulls you back from a poor course, accepting the warning may prevent greater trouble.
What it means: A partner, rule, or real-world limit may be guiding you back toward safer ground.
Try: Understand what the resistance is revealing and coordinate with reliable people.
Avoid: Treating every limit as an attempt to suppress you.

In short: Broken equipment and a divided relationship show that your method is carrying too much strain.
What it means: A wheel coming off and spouses turning against each other both point to failed internal connections.
Try: Repair tools, processes, and communication before demanding more speed.
Avoid: Pushing the schedule while ignoring fractures in the partnership.

In short: Speaking honestly about the danger can allow fear and harm to recede.
What it means: Sincerity creates a route away from bloodshed and anxiety because people no longer need to guess what is hidden.
Try: Use concrete facts and acknowledge the responsibility that is yours.
Avoid: Concealing the problem or calming people with vague promises.

In short: Genuine cooperation allows resources to be shared without constant suspicion.
What it means: Neighbors prospering together shows how trust can gather many small strengths into something useful.
Try: Create a transparent way to distribute benefits among contributors.
Avoid: Treating the results of cooperation as private property.

In short: The long-awaited rain has come, but pressing for more may now upset the balance.
What it means: A moon near full will soon begin to wane; maturity brings results but less room for reckless expansion.
Try: Consolidate gains, stabilize the system, and let the team recover.
Avoid: Doubling the risk immediately because success feels exciting.

gua: Hexagram 10 is like walking beside a tiger's tail: you are moving near power, rules, or real danger. Progress may still be possible, but each step requires respect, boundaries, and attention to the other party's response. Caution is not cowardice; it is informed action. Looking back at how you walked reveals where the outcome truly came from.
lines:
In short: A plain, direct beginning creates the least unnecessary burden.
What it means: Walking simply means relying on real ability rather than status, decoration, or special treatment.
Try: Do the basic work well and establish your place through results.
Avoid: Beginning with display, entitlement, or expensive appearances.

In short: On a clear road, quiet focus will carry you steadily forward.
What it means: The solitary person is not fleeing society; they are refusing to let noise and comparison dictate their pace.
Try: Maintain a consistent rhythm and decide independently what suits you.
Avoid: Changing a reasonable pace simply because others are accelerating.

In short: Challenging a powerful force while unable to see or move well is extremely risky.
What it means: The lame person walking and the blind person looking show courage being used to deny genuine limitations.
Try: Assess the gaps honestly, seek assistance, or reduce the scope.
Avoid: Calling recklessness bravery.

In short: Knowing that you are near danger and staying alert can help you pass safely.
What it means: The tiger's tail is still being stepped on, but awareness of consequences changes how each move is made.
Try: Prepare contingencies and watch the response to every step.
Avoid: Assuming that one lucky escape proves the situation is safe.

In short: Decisive action may be needed, but forceful decisions still carry danger.
What it means: A firm tread concentrates authority and speed, which can move matters forward while also injuring people.
Try: Explain your reasoning, set review points, and hear credible objections.
Avoid: Treating disagreement as disloyalty or valuing speed above safety.

In short: Review the effects of the whole journey before calling the result complete.
What it means: Examining your steps means judging not only whether the goal was reached, but also the methods and cost.
Try: Review decisions, relationships, and unfinished responsibilities.
Avoid: Using success to excuse damage left behind.

gua: Hexagram 11 depicts open exchange between above and below, with people and resources moving freely. This is a favorable time to cooperate, clear obstacles, and turn good conditions into stable results. Yet ease does not maintain itself. The more successful the situation becomes, the more important it is to notice neglected details and quieter voices.
lines:
In short: Pulling one blade of grass brings others with it; move together with people who share the purpose.
What it means: Your choice affects connected partners and can create momentum beyond one person.
Try: Coordinate with aligned allies before beginning.
Avoid: Rushing ahead alone and leaving your partners unprepared.

In short: A thriving situation needs enough breadth to include rough edges, distance, and overlooked people.
What it means: Good leadership does not serve only the polished center; it carries imperfections and reaches beyond familiar circles.
Try: Balance efficiency with fairness and seek input from people at the margins.
Avoid: Designing everything around the loudest group.

In short: Even the flattest road has slopes; accepting change in good times prevents panic later.
What it means: Growth and decline are normal cycles, not proof that all previous effort failed.
Try: Keep reserves and backup plans while appreciating the present.
Avoid: Spending the future on the assumption that favorable conditions will last.

In short: Dropping display and suspicion can narrow the distance between different ranks.
What it means: People moving freely without relying on wealth suggests that genuine contact matters more than ceremony.
Try: Communicate directly and share information as equals.
Avoid: Using rank, gifts, or privilege to purchase loyalty.

In short: A well-formed alliance can build trust between groups that were once separate.
What it means: A royal marriage symbolizes a formal connection serving more than private happiness.
Try: Make the partnership clear, mutual, and respectful of everyone involved.
Avoid: Treating relationships as bargaining chips.

In short: When the walls begin collapsing, secure the interior instead of launching another campaign.
What it means: A wall falling into its moat shows that old defenses and commands no longer function.
Try: Stop expanding, repair the system, and acknowledge the disorder honestly.
Avoid: Covering a weak foundation with forceful slogans.

gua: Hexagram 12 describes blocked communication, when worthwhile ideas struggle to reach the people who can act on them. Institutions and human needs may be moving apart, and good work may not receive quick recognition. Preserve your capability rather than surrendering your standards merely to fit in. A closed period will eventually shift, but someone must rebuild the connections.
lines:
In short: When the environment deteriorates, one person's departure or resistance may affect the whole group.
What it means: Pulling grass up by the roots shows how people with shared values remain connected.
Try: Discuss limits with trusted allies and coordinate your response.
Avoid: Escaping privately while leaving all the risk to others.

In short: Within a poor system, temporary endurance may protect you, but it should not erase the larger purpose.
What it means: Tolerance need not mean approval; it can be a way to avoid wasteful confrontation while preserving what matters.
Try: Protect basic needs, keep records, and retain the possibility of change.
Avoid: Gradually accepting wrongdoing merely for comfort.

In short: Hiding a problem to preserve appearances eventually produces shame.
What it means: Wrapping up the disgrace does not resolve it; it only keeps it out of sight.
Try: Admit the mistake, repair the harm, and restore transparency.
Avoid: Telling new lies to protect the first one.

In short: When purpose, authority, and support become clear, the blockage can begin to loosen.
What it means: Useful reform now has more behind it than one person's impatience.
Try: Confirm your mandate and work with reliable people on the central obstruction.
Avoid: Dressing personal ambition up as a public mission.

In short: When conditions begin to improve, secure the change so that the old problem does not return.
What it means: Tying the situation firmly to a mulberry tree suggests that one apparent recovery is not enough.
Try: Build monitoring, backup, and clear accountability.
Avoid: Removing every safeguard at the first sign of relief.

In short: Stagnation can finally collapse, though the shift may feel unsettling before it feels joyful.
What it means: The old closed structure may loosen suddenly, bringing uncertainty along with opportunity.
Try: Use the opening to restore communication and cooperation quickly.
Avoid: Rejecting a new possibility solely because the past was disappointing.

gua: Hexagram 13 concerns cooperation across familiar boundaries. True allies need not share the same background, but they must be able to communicate openly and follow common rules. Private factions create suspicion; honest differences can support a broader alliance. In conflict, look first for a shared task rather than demanding personal closeness.
lines:
In short: Cooperation that begins openly is less likely to create suspicion.
What it means: Meeting at the gate means the arrangement can be seen and is not built on a hidden bargain.
Try: Make the invitation, conditions, and decision process public.
Avoid: Forming an inner group first and later demanding everyone else's obedience.

In short: Staying only with your own clan makes the circle of cooperation smaller and smaller.
What it means: When kinship or faction replaces shared principles, outsiders have little reason to trust the group.
Try: Choose partners by ability and values, including people from different backgrounds.
Avoid: Favoritism and treating dissenters as enemies.

In short: Hidden weapons and prolonged surveillance show that mistrust has already stopped cooperation.
What it means: An alliance that prepares secretly for battle will interpret every small event as a threat.
Try: Reduce hostility and use a neutral process to discuss the real risks.
Avoid: Collecting leverage, testing loyalty, or preparing a surprise attack.

In short: Even from a strong position, choosing not to attack can be the more mature victory.
What it means: You may be able to climb the wall and strike, yet see that conflict would cost more than winning.
Try: Keep enough strength to hold a boundary and propose a ceasefire.
Avoid: Assuming that power must be used simply because you possess it.

In short: Real allies may first be separated by misunderstanding, then reunite after the barrier is cleared.
What it means: Crying before laughing shows that repair comes through difficulty, not through never having disagreed.
Try: Verify the facts, express what matters, and allow a sincere attempt at repair.
Avoid: Testing whether the other person understands you by remaining silent.

In short: A meeting at the outskirts may not be intimate, but it can preserve room for cooperation.
What it means: The relationship remains peripheral—without deep commitment, yet also without direct harm.
Try: Accept limited cooperation and let trust grow from experience.
Avoid: Pressuring the other party to become a close ally immediately.

gua: Hexagram 14 points to abundant resources, talent, or achievement. The real test is not how to possess more, but how to use what you have fairly and sustainably. Abundance amplifies character: sharing attracts cooperation, while control breeds resentment. Large undertakings are possible, provided decisions remain transparent and measured.
lines:
In short: When resources first arrive, establish a way to use them without display or harm.
What it means: You have not yet entered complicated entanglements, so healthy rules are still easy to create.
Try: Set budgets, responsibilities, and risk limits early.
Avoid: Using a new advantage to dominate others or spend impulsively.

In short: Like a large wagon built for weight, you can now carry a larger undertaking.
What it means: Resources are not only plentiful; the system can also move and support them.
Try: Integrate people, tools, and processes around a worthwhile long-term goal.
Avoid: Looking only at total resources while ignoring carrying and maintenance capacity.

In short: People with surplus can direct resources toward a larger public good.
What it means: Offering something to the ruler symbolizes connecting private achievement to wider needs, not flattering authority.
Try: Support the team, community, or long-term infrastructure.
Avoid: Treating generosity as publicity or demanding immediate repayment.

In short: Refusing to compare and display your abundance helps preserve clear judgment.
What it means: Quiet prosperity attracts less envy and reduces the pressure to expand merely for appearance.
Try: Focus on what the resources are for and strengthen weak areas discreetly.
Avoid: Using grandeur to prove that you have succeeded.

In short: Sincerity combined with appropriate authority can unite people.
What it means: Warmth without boundaries may lose direction, while authority without trust creates fear.
Try: Keep promises openly and enforce clear rules consistently.
Avoid: Trying to please everyone or suddenly resorting to intimidation.

In short: When support comes from many directions, remain grateful and responsible.
What it means: Help from heaven is not a guarantee of luck; your conduct may simply make people willing to assist.
Try: Credit others, give back, and continue using resources responsibly.
Avoid: Explaining every success as personal ability alone.

gua: Hexagram 15 presents humility as an accurate knowledge of both strengths and limits, not self-belittlement. A mountain hidden within the earth has real substance without needing to stand above everyone. Humility makes cooperation easier and preserves room to learn. When responsibility calls for firmness, modesty need not mean retreat.
lines:
In short: Grounded humility gives you the capacity to cross a difficult river.
What it means: Deep modesty is not low confidence; it is thorough preparation without underestimating the task.
Try: Focus on the work and consult people who understand the terrain.
Avoid: Exaggerating your certainty to protect an image.

In short: When others can feel your humility, it probably comes from consistent behavior.
What it means: A good reputation need not be self-advertised; respect and reliability become visible over time.
Try: Keep listening, share credit, and receive reasonable praise without discomfort.
Avoid: Performing modesty or denying abilities you actually have.

In short: Achievement combined with continued effort earns durable respect.
What it means: Hardworking humility joins proven results with a refusal to become entitled.
Try: Finish the final details and prepare others to carry the work forward.
Avoid: Demanding special privileges after success.

In short: Humility should become action that adjusts position, attention, and resources.
What it means: Active modesty is more than polite language; it deliberately makes cooperation easier.
Try: Let the right people speak and give opportunities to those equipped for them.
Avoid: Speaking modestly while retaining all control.

In short: Do not use wealth or power to pressure neighbors, but do address genuine disorder.
What it means: Humility does not cancel the duty to lead or to defend necessary boundaries.
Try: Begin with communication and clear rules, then use proportionate measures if needed.
Avoid: Making force the first response or avoiding every conflict.

In short: When a problem becomes public, begin the correction within your own camp.
What it means: Mobilizing after humility is not a license to conquer outsiders; it calls for internal discipline.
Try: Examine your team, habits, and neglected responsibilities first.
Avoid: Assigning every cause to an external enemy.

gua: Hexagram 16 concerns enthusiasm, preparation, and collective momentum. Hope can inspire participation, but excitement can also hide practical weaknesses. Arrange the structure, pace, and support before the energy peaks, so enthusiasm becomes more than a brief display. Useful joy tells people why they are moving and how the effort can continue.
lines:
In short: Advertising your excitement too early can make your promises outrun your ability.
What it means: Joy that immediately cries out may be seeking outside approval before anything solid exists.
Try: Produce an initial result before announcing the larger plan.
Avoid: Presenting an aspiration as a guarantee.

In short: Notice the first sign of change and adjust without waiting another day.
What it means: Being firm as stone yet quick to read conditions means excitement has not clouded your judgment.
Try: Set objective indicators and respond promptly when they move.
Avoid: Hiding concerns because everyone else is celebrating.

In short: Watching someone else's success while hesitating over your own path leads to regret.
What it means: When your enthusiasm depends on an authority's expression, you surrender your timing.
Try: Confirm your own aim and begin what is already within reach.
Avoid: Waiting indefinitely for someone else to authorize or lead you.

In short: You can become a source of constructive energy, drawing many sincere partners together.
What it means: Like a pin gathering strands of hair, a central figure can connect otherwise scattered strengths.
Try: Offer a credible direction and let participants share in the results.
Avoid: Distrusting everyone or treating collective enthusiasm as personal property.

In short: A persistent difficulty that does not destroy you needs management rather than panic.
What it means: A chronic illness can resemble ongoing stress, a system flaw, or exhaustion that allows work to continue at a cost.
Try: Create a plan for care and repair, and reduce unnecessary load.
Avoid: Pretending nothing is wrong or abandoning everything after one difficult period.

In short: Excitement can become intoxicating, but changing course once you notice it is not too late.
What it means: Darkened enthusiasm resembles excess entertainment or collective frenzy that has lost proportion.
Try: Step away from the stimulation and reassess the cost and direction.
Avoid: Refusing to sober up because you have already invested heavily.

gua: Hexagram 17 concerns following, adapting, and moving with others. Following does not mean surrendering judgment; it means deciding what deserves trust and joining its rhythm. Choosing one companion often means leaving another path behind, so look beyond immediate advantage to shared values. A mature follower can offer honest advice, and a mature leader does not depend on coercion.
lines:
In short: Your role or direction is changing; contact with people outside your usual circle will help.
What it means: A change in office means old habits may no longer fit and a fresh perspective is needed.
Try: Exchange views openly and clarify your new responsibility.
Avoid: Listening only to familiar voices while the change remains merely nominal.

In short: Grasping a small immediate benefit may cost you a more mature partnership.
What it means: Holding on to the child while losing the adult suggests choosing someone easy to control but limited in ability.
Try: Select partners for long-term value.
Avoid: Excluding capable people because they may challenge you.

In short: Following mature strength may end a childish dependence and bring you closer to what you seek.
What it means: Leaving the lesser for the greater means raising both your standards and your willingness to carry responsibility.
Try: Choose cooperation that helps you grow and state your needs clearly.
Avoid: Losing integrity after gaining access to resources.

In short: Success through close alignment can attract suspicion, so your motives must remain visible.
What it means: Rapid gains or proximity to power may make others wonder whether you follow only for advantage.
Try: Disclose principles, interests, and reasons for decisions.
Avoid: Private exchanges of favors or unquestioning loyalty.

In short: Sincere devotion to a worthy value gives following a steady direction.
What it means: What you trust is a quality worth practicing, not merely the charm of one person.
Try: Turn the value into daily choices and let companions hold you accountable.
Avoid: Treating an admired person as incapable of error.

In short: A deep commitment can unite people strongly, but make sure it has not become a chain.
What it means: Binding and maintaining a bond suggests the pressure of a long alliance, mission, or tradition.
Try: Renew the shared purpose and ensure the commitment remains voluntary.
Avoid: Using gratitude, custom, or moral pressure to forbid departure.

gua: Hexagram 18 faces neglect, corruption, or inherited problems that have accumulated over time. Repair requires more than blaming previous generations, but it also cannot pretend everything can instantly begin anew. Learn how the damage developed, address its consequences, and create systems that prevent recurrence. Leave time before and after the intervention so that reform goes deeper than surface cleaning.
lines:
In short: You may inherit an older generation's mistake and still repair both the damage and its reputation.
What it means: A parent's disorder may appear as debt, habits, or a broken institution; inheritance does not require repetition.
Try: Find the root, take responsibility for what is yours, and establish a new practice.
Avoid: Blaming the past alone or hiding it to protect appearances.

In short: Old problems within care and family bonds need a gentler form of correction.
What it means: Emotional dependence is often more sensitive than a procedural error, and a harsh break can create fresh harm.
Try: Understand the background and adjust attachment and boundaries gradually.
Avoid: Shaming someone into immediate change.

In short: Reforming an inherited problem may bring some regret, but neglect would be worse.
What it means: Change disturbs established habits; the method may be imperfect even when the direction is necessary.
Try: Address the main harm first and improve the approach as you proceed.
Avoid: Preserving the status quo because criticism feels uncomfortable.

In short: Excessive tolerance of an old problem will eventually become difficult to defend.
What it means: Treating corruption as a private family matter lets the consequences continue spreading.
Try: Set deadlines and responsibilities, and stop providing unconditional rescue.
Avoid: Using loyalty, filial duty, or harmony as a reason to have no limits.

In short: Repairing the failures of the past in a verifiable way can restore trust.
What it means: This is not polishing history, but recovering damaged credibility through visible results.
Try: Make the improvements measurable and credit everyone who helped.
Avoid: Turning collective repair into a personal hero story.

In short: After inherited duties are settled, you may choose a life beyond the old centers of power.
What it means: Refusing service to rulers can mean leaving competition behind to preserve independence and higher values.
Try: Complete the necessary handover, then choose a path consistent with your principles.
Avoid: Dressing an escape from unfinished responsibility as moral purity.

gua: Hexagram 19 describes approaching people, opportunity, or a new responsibility. You may be moving closer to a team, a leadership role, or someone who needs support. Good leadership learns conditions firsthand while offering both care and boundaries. A rising period can seem spacious, but this is precisely when to plan for what remains after the excitement passes.
lines:
In short: Mutual interest creates a good opening for cooperation based on shared principles.
What it means: Both sides are approaching; this is not one person chasing another.
Try: Confirm the common aim and build trust through small commitments.
Avoid: Skipping essential conversations because the mood is warm.

In short: Continued mutual response allows the relationship to handle more substantial work.
What it means: A second approach shows that cooperation has begun to take form.
Try: Share information and decisions so that both sides genuinely participate.
Avoid: Enjoying support while denying the other person influence.

In short: Sweet talk alone cannot sustain closeness for long.
What it means: Pleasant promises may attract people temporarily while leaving actual problems untouched.
Try: Stop flattering, acknowledge what is wrong, and correct it directly.
Avoid: Trading promises for short-term approval.

In short: Reaching the actual scene allows a fuller and more accurate judgment.
What it means: Complete approach means understanding the need firsthand instead of through rank and assumption.
Try: Meet the people doing the work and direct resources toward the real issue.
Avoid: Relying only on reports or one gatekeeper.

In short: Wise leadership knows both when to intervene and when to delegate.
What it means: Understanding the whole does not require controlling every detail.
Try: Select capable people, give a clear objective, and review at sensible intervals.
Avoid: Micromanagement or complete neglect.

In short: Approaching people with generosity and a long view creates durable influence.
What it means: Deep approach is not a burst of warmth; it accepts responsibility for what follows.
Try: Continue the support and turn personal help into a reliable system.
Avoid: Disappearing as soon as appreciation has been won.

gua: Hexagram 20 concerns observation and the way your own conduct is observed. Step back before deciding so that patterns, effects, and the larger context become visible. In leadership, ordinary behavior teaches more than slogans. Observation has little value if it remains passive; eventually it must become self-examination and action.
lines:
In short: A child's surface view is harmless in small matters but inadequate for major decisions.
What it means: Limited information and a narrow angle make it easy to mistake a first impression for the whole.
Try: Ask about sources, context, and other viewpoints.
Avoid: Drawing a major conclusion from a fragment.

In short: Looking through a crack gives only a partial view, so keep your judgment provisional.
What it means: Status, fear, or controlled information may be restricting what you can see.
Try: Admit what remains unknown and widen your sources before committing.
Avoid: Treating limited experience as a universal truth.

In short: Your own pattern of living can tell you whether to advance or withdraw.
What it means: Evidence lies not only in outside signs, but also in your long-term energy, results, and well-being.
Try: Review records and identify what consistently develops or depletes you.
Avoid: Deciding only from today's mood.

In short: See how an organization truly operates before deciding whether to join it.
What it means: Observing a country's splendor includes its culture, systems, and treatment of people—not just visible success.
Try: Speak to insiders and experience the environment directly.
Avoid: Being won over by brand or authority alone.

In short: A leader should study how their own conduct affects everyone else.
What it means: The outcome acts as a mirror for whether policy and personal example agree.
Try: Gather candid feedback and correct acknowledged blind spots.
Avoid: Requiring reflection from everyone except yourself.

In short: The greater your influence, the more your long pattern of conduct must withstand scrutiny.
What it means: People observe not one performance, but how power has been used over time.
Try: Clarify the principles worth passing on and accept outside examination.
Avoid: Using reputation to cover unfinished obligations.

gua: Hexagram 21 is like biting through something hard lodged between the teeth. Progress is blocked by misconduct, concealment, or a rule that has not been enforced. Investigation and proportionate consequences are needed. Excessive leniency prolongs the problem, while excessive punishment creates a new injustice; the purpose is to restore normal function, not to enjoy condemnation.
lines:
In short: A small, clear restriction at the first mistake can prevent a worse course.
What it means: A wooden restraint covering the toes limits movement while the harm is still slight.
Try: State the rule and consequence promptly, then allow correction.
Avoid: Ignoring early misconduct and later responding with extreme punishment.

In short: Even an easy case can be handled too forcefully.
What it means: Biting tender meat until the nose is buried suggests that being right does not remove the need for proportion.
Try: Address the act rather than attacking the person, and review the impact afterward.
Avoid: Humiliating someone because the evidence favors you.

In short: Investigating an old problem may expose something unpleasant, but the work can still be completed.
What it means: Poison in aged meat resembles an inherited case, corruption, or a hidden truth.
Try: Protect yourself, preserve evidence, and follow a sound process.
Avoid: Covering the matter again because it is embarrassing or repellent.

In short: A deeply embedded problem requires dependable tools and firm principles.
What it means: Metal found inside a dry bone suggests a hard obstruction with evidence buried within it.
Try: Use professional standards and accept that the process may be demanding.
Avoid: Judging by emotion or bypassing procedure for speed.

In short: Finding decisive evidence in a difficult case does not remove the need for care.
What it means: Gold within dried meat represents a reliable core discovered in complicated circumstances.
Try: Cross-check the evidence and make the conclusion open to review.
Avoid: Ending the investigation as soon as one favorable fact appears.

In short: Repeatedly refusing to listen can eventually make every warning inaudible.
What it means: A heavy restraint covering the ears symbolizes both serious consequences and a closed mind.
Try: Stop before the consequences grow and seek feedback capable of reaching you.
Avoid: Interpreting all criticism as hostility.

gua: Hexagram 22 concerns beauty, presentation, and the value of form. Good form helps content become understandable, just as clear design helps a message travel. Appearance cannot replace substance, however. Decoration can assist smaller matters, while important direction must still come from real value; in the end, simplicity and sincerity often last longest.
lines:
In short: It may be better to walk under your own power than ride in an unsuitable splendid carriage.
What it means: Adorning the feet and leaving the carriage suggests choosing a simple method consistent with your real position.
Try: Rely on your ability and preserve freedom of movement.
Avoid: Accepting status or convenience whose hidden cost is too high.

In short: A beard depends on the chin; presentation should follow substance.
What it means: Decoration has no independent value when it does not serve a genuine subject.
Try: Build the product, argument, or relationship first, then improve its presentation.
Avoid: Investing more effort in packaging content that is not there.

In short: Attractive success still needs an inner standard that can be maintained.
What it means: A glossy, rain-washed appearance draws praise and can also tempt you to become absorbed in it.
Try: Enjoy recognition while continuing to inspect quality.
Avoid: Hiding faults to preserve a polished image.

In short: Before judging, find out whether what approaches is honest simplicity or aggressive display.
What it means: The swiftly arriving white horse is not a robber but a sincere suitor; unfamiliarity can resemble threat.
Try: Understand the intention before choosing your response.
Avoid: Rejecting goodwill solely because of its appearance.

In short: A modest gift offered sincerely can still establish a valuable relationship.
What it means: A small roll of silk in the garden looks meager, but its worth lies in being genuine and proportionate.
Try: Express care within your means and make the intention clear.
Avoid: Borrowing or pretending in order to maintain appearances.

In short: Removing excess decoration and returning to the essence creates freedom from regret.
What it means: Plain white beauty is not emptiness; it is mature content no longer needing exaggeration.
Try: Remove inflated promises and needless complexity.
Avoid: Turning simplicity into another calculated display.

gua: Hexagram 23 is like plaster peeling from a wall layer by layer: the supports of an existing situation are diminishing. This is not the time to force expansion. Identify which people, resources, and methods can no longer carry weight. Stop the damaged parts and preserve the essential core; decline is not permanent, but a new beginning needs honest space.
lines:
In short: The damage has begun at the foundation; early repair will cost the least.
What it means: Rot reaching the bed's feet resembles small cracks in basic process, finance, or trust.
Try: Inspect the fundamentals and repair one fault that will otherwise spread.
Avoid: Assuming the foundation is sound because the surface still functions.

In short: Decay has spread from the bed's feet into its frame and can no longer be treated as minor.
What it means: The supporting structure now resembles damaged roles, daily order, or relationship rules.
Try: Reduce scope, stop nonessential commitments, and clarify duties and boundaries.
Avoid: Using temporary patches to preserve the old appearance.

In short: Even when everything around you is deteriorating, you need not follow the same wrong course.
What it means: Separating from an unhealthy group can feel isolating, but leaving a harmful consensus is not betrayal.
Try: Keep independent judgment and withdraw from conduct you know is damaging.
Avoid: Joining what you oppose merely to belong to the majority.

In short: The damage now touches your immediate well-being, so loss must be stopped at once.
What it means: A bed worn through to the skin points to consequences affecting health, safety, income, or dignity.
Try: Protect those affected and pause, seek help, or leave if necessary.
Avoid: Remaining exposed because of pride or sunk cost.

In short: Bringing scattered people into a fair order can stabilize the situation.
What it means: Fish strung in sequence suggest resources, information, and care reaching everyone in turn.
Try: Create a clear order and equitable access.
Avoid: Favoring a few or forcing everyone to compete for one exit.

In short: Even amid decline, one mature fruit can be preserved as seed for the future.
What it means: Saving the uneaten fruit means retaining the people, knowledge, credibility, or funds from which renewal can grow.
Try: Protect the essential core for the next phase.
Avoid: Consuming the last reserves for temporary comfort.

gua: Hexagram 24 is the return to a sound course after wandering away. It does not demand instant perfection, only a willingness to turn when you notice the deviation. Rest, reflection, and the restoration of ordinary rhythm all belong to returning. An error corrected early does not have to become a lasting burden.
lines:
In short: Noticing a small deviation and returning immediately is the least costly correction.
What it means: Turning back soon after taking the wrong road requires changed action, not prolonged self-punishment.
Try: Correct the message, decision, or habit now.
Avoid: Letting embarrassment make a simple repair complicated.

In short: Returning with safe, kind support is easier than forcing yourself back alone.
What it means: A healthy example or reliable friend can naturally restore a steadier rhythm.
Try: Stay near people who help you become calm, honest, and capable.
Avoid: Treating help as weakness or returning to the environment that repeatedly destabilizes you.

In short: Repeatedly straying and returning is tiring, but correction remains better than surrender.
What it means: Recurrence can weaken confidence while also revealing the triggers more clearly each time.
Try: Record the situations surrounding relapse and design smaller steps or a different environment.
Avoid: Depending only on vows of willpower and condemning your whole self after failure.

In short: Even when companions go the other way, you can return to the better direction alone.
What it means: Independent return means testing the group against conscience, safety, and fact.
Try: Examine majority opinion and state a different choice when needed.
Avoid: Putting belonging ahead of long-term welfare.

In short: A sincere, steady return does not require endless punishment.
What it means: Deep return is shown by new choices repeatedly appearing in daily life, not by dramatic declarations.
Try: Establish sustainable routines and repair trust through action.
Avoid: Becoming absorbed in regret while changing nothing practical.

In short: Refusing to turn back for too long can eventually damage the ability to correct course.
What it means: A lost leader who keeps marching the group onward turns a private error into a collective disaster.
Try: Require outside review before major decisions and allow trusted people to expose blind spots.
Avoid: Investing more resources in a mistake merely to preserve authority.

gua: Hexagram 25 asks you to reduce calculation and act from facts and proper responsibility. It does not promise that innocent people will never meet accidents; it says not to create extra trouble through distorted methods. When something unexpected occurs, separate your responsibility from coincidence. Sincerity still needs judgment—good intentions do not replace risk management.
lines:
In short: When your purpose is straightforward and your method sound, you can move ahead with confidence.
What it means: Acting without falseness is not charging ahead without a plan; it is refusing to be diverted by vanity, suspicion, or manipulation.
Try: State the purpose and make one direct decision based on current facts.
Avoid: Setting traps, testing people, or exaggerating in order to control their response.

In short: Do the work in front of you without calculating a reward at every step.
What it means: Plowing without fixating on harvest means giving the process proper attention instead of letting hunger for results damage it.
Try: Focus on controllable effort, quality, and pace.
Avoid: Changing direction whenever rewards are delayed or using shortcuts that bypass preparation.

In short: Some losses are not your fault, but their consequences still require calm attention.
What it means: A passerby steals a tethered cow and nearby residents are blamed; accidents can draw innocent people into suspicion.
Try: Preserve evidence, establish the timeline, and address the loss before assigning blame.
Avoid: Searching for a convenient scapegoat or reacting destructively out of indignation.

In short: Reliable principles keep confusion from creating additional mistakes.
What it means: Even when events are unpredictable, you can retain basic standards of honesty, contract, and safety.
Try: Return to tested procedures and promises that will withstand later review.
Avoid: Breaking effective boundaries to relieve immediate pressure.

In short: Not every discomfort needs aggressive treatment; observation may sometimes be wiser.
What it means: An illness arising without misconduct may ease on its own, while unnecessary intervention creates a new burden.
Try: Check whether the issue is self-limiting, use low-risk care, and keep watching it.
Avoid: Letting anxiety dictate treatment—or using an oracle instead of professional medical care.

In short: Sincere intentions can still cause harm when conditions are wrong.
What it means: Goodwill does not erase missing information, poor timing, or other people's boundaries.
Try: Pause expansion, gather facts, and ask those who will be affected.
Avoid: Using “I meant well” to override reality or consent.

gua: Hexagram 26 is the accumulation of great strength without spending it all at once. True reserves include skill, discipline, relationships, and judgment, not resources alone. A temporary restriction may be preventing immature power from running out of control. Once preparation is sufficient, what you have stored can support a much larger challenge.
lines:
In short: A clear danger lies ahead; stopping now is not retreat.
What it means: A horse ready to run sees a hazardous road, and braking preserves its strength for better conditions.
Try: Pause high-risk investment and identify missing skill, permission, or protection.
Avoid: Calling impulse courage and accelerating past visible warnings.

In short: When the axle is loose, forcing the wagon forward causes greater damage.
What it means: A critical connection is missing from the tools, people, or process on which the plan depends.
Try: Accept the temporary halt and repair the central connection first.
Avoid: Demanding speed while ignoring the cause of system failure.

In short: A fine horse can run, but the carriage and defenses still need practice.
What it means: Strong momentum and rising opportunity bring greater risk as well as greater capacity.
Try: Stress-test the plan, rehearse roles, and prepare backups before accelerating.
Avoid: Skipping training because ability has improved.

In short: Guidance works best before the young ox grows dangerous horns.
What it means: Early restraint resembles teaching good rules and habits while a problem remains small, rather than punishing it later.
Try: Set clear boundaries early and explain why they exist.
Avoid: Allowing matters to become unmanageable before intervening harshly.

In short: Changing the source of the boar's aggression is more effective than merely blunting its tusks.
What it means: Lasting safety comes from reducing the drive that produces danger, not repeatedly treating its visible tool.
Try: Address incentives, system gaps, or unmet needs behind the conflict.
Avoid: Banning symptoms while preserving the environment that creates them.

In short: Long preparation now opens onto a broad road where your ability can serve a larger field.
What it means: The great highway symbolizes self-control and training becoming genuine freedom of choice.
Try: Choose a direction that uses your strengths and benefits more people.
Avoid: Scattering your attention as soon as freedom arrives.

gua: Hexagram 27 concerns how you nourish yourself and others—with food, information, emotion, and speech. What you consume each day gradually shapes you. Before caring for others, examine whether your sources are healthy and your method sustainable. Words are also nourishment: they can strengthen people or injure them.
lines:
In short: Ignoring your own resources while envying someone else's leaves you without direction.
What it means: Abandoning the self-sustaining sacred tortoise to stare at another person's mouth is an image of comparison replacing self-knowledge.
Try: Inventory your skills, relationships, and choices before deciding what is truly missing.
Avoid: Treating another person's life as the answer to yours.

In short: Seeking support from an unsuitable source can turn need into dangerous dependence.
What it means: The normal direction of nourishment has been reversed, leaving responsibilities and needs badly matched.
Try: Restructure income, care, or emotional support around a more balanced arrangement.
Avoid: Placing every survival need on someone unable or unfit to carry it.

In short: Persisting in a harmful way of meeting a need will not make it healthy.
What it means: Nourishment has been distorted into addiction, manipulation, overwork, or a diet of damaging information.
Try: Admit the method is hurting you and seek professional support and replacement habits.
Avoid: Calling years of endurance perseverance when nothing improves.

In short: Seeking resources actively is legitimate when your purpose is clear and your method fair.
What it means: The tiger's intense gaze shows that powerful need can become disciplined care rather than predation.
Try: State what is needed, follow up consistently, and protect fairness.
Avoid: Using urgency to seize, flatter, or pressure.

In short: When capacity is limited, maintaining ordinary life is wiser than taking on a huge crossing.
What it means: Existing support may sustain you, but you are not yet ready to bear a major risk independently.
Try: Preserve core work, learn from reliable people, and build autonomy gradually.
Avoid: Taking on an oversized responsibility merely to prove independence.

In short: Becoming a source of nourishment for others is both an honor and a high-risk responsibility.
What it means: Many people may depend on you for resources, direction, or care.
Try: Create transparent distribution and self-care systems so everything does not rest on you.
Avoid: Enjoying the power of being needed while neglecting your limits and others' growth.

gua: Hexagram 28 is like a roof beam carrying more weight than usual. The situation can no longer be handled in the ordinary way; pressure may come from excessive duty, a hard deadline, or structural imbalance. Exceptional measures may be required, but they still need safety limits. Find what is most likely to break, then reinforce it, share the load, or leave.
lines:
In short: The more important the matter, the more worthwhile an extra layer of protection at the beginning.
What it means: Laying clean white grass beneath an offering may look overcautious, but it protects something precious.
Try: Add checks, backups, and buffer time to critical delivery.
Avoid: Dismissing details until a small mistake ruins major work.

In short: New growth can emerge from an exhausted place, and an unusual pairing may restore life.
What it means: An old willow sprouting and an older man marrying a young woman symbolize old resources renewed by fresh energy.
Try: Combine experience with a new perspective and assess real complementarity.
Avoid: Rejecting an unconventional partnership—or overlooking unequal power.

In short: When the main support is bending, further weight may bring the whole structure down.
What it means: This is not ordinary fatigue but danger to a key person, cash supply, or essential system.
Try: Reduce the load immediately, divide responsibility, and obtain expert review.
Avoid: Demanding that the support keep standing through motivation alone.

In short: A reinforced beam can carry weight, but side commitments may bend it again.
What it means: The core has recovered while private interests and extra obligations remain potential burdens.
Try: Keep priorities clear and accept only work compatible with the repair.
Avoid: Filling all available capacity the moment conditions improve.

In short: A flash of blossom on a withered tree is not the same as durable fruit.
What it means: An unlikely late flowering may be enjoyable without being sustainable or widely accepted.
Try: Appreciate the result while asking whether it can create value for the next stage.
Avoid: Mistaking one burst of attention, sales, or passion for stable renewal.

In short: Crossing until the water covers your head may be brave, but it can also be fatal.
What it means: A rescue effort or ideal has swallowed the person attempting it; good motives do not erase the cost.
Try: Set an exit line and rescue plan, and accept only risks you can truly bear.
Avoid: Using self-sacrifice to justify an adventure without limits.

gua: Hexagram 29 is a succession of pits: danger is recurring rather than isolated. The task is not to leap over everything through force, but to read the terrain and steady both mind and action. Honest recognition of risk reduces the secondary harm caused by panic. Handling one manageable section at a time is still progress.
lines:
In short: You may be falling into the same problem again; do not force your way onward.
What it means: Repeated pits suggest that the difficulty may partly come from using the same ineffective method.
Try: Stop, identify the risk, seek help, and change the approach.
Avoid: Investing more time or resources merely to prove yourself.

In short: While inside danger, seek one small gain rather than imagining a single dramatic escape.
What it means: A foothold in the pit preserves confidence and resources.
Try: Reduce the goal to one safe step you can complete today.
Avoid: Staking everything on a reversal or dismissing small improvements.

In short: With danger before and behind and no safe place to rest, postpone action.
What it means: Using danger as a pillow shows exhaustion lowering awareness until the abnormal feels ordinary.
Try: Delay major decisions, recover strength, and gather information.
Avoid: Gambling everything at peak pressure or treating familiar danger as safety.

In short: In an emergency, simple and sincere cooperation matters more than ceremony.
What it means: Modest food and drink passed through a window show limited resources opening a route through trust.
Try: Communicate directly and request one concrete, manageable form of help.
Avoid: Refusing help because it is not impressive enough or making grand empty promises.

In short: The pit is not filled, but the water is nearing balance; stabilize before celebrating.
What it means: The danger remains, though its most violent imbalance has eased.
Try: Continue monitoring and repair the remaining gaps.
Avoid: Removing safeguards and charging forward at the first improvement.

In short: Long confinement by accumulated mistakes may require outside help and time.
What it means: Bound with heavy rope among thorns, you may no longer be able to undo the consequences alone.
Try: Accept responsibility and seek legal, financial, medical, or other trusted support.
Avoid: Concealment, escape, or expecting the same failed method to work.

gua: Hexagram 30 is fire and light, but also dependence: fire needs fuel, and people depend on values, relationships, and institutions. Healthy dependence lets both sides shine; uncontrolled attachment burns rapidly. See clearly what sustains you and maintain the light in a gentle, steady way. Once a problem is visible, do not let emotional fire dictate the response.
lines:
In short: When the first signs are tangled, slowing down prevents a costly misstep.
What it means: Crossing footprints show that the direction is not yet clear.
Try: Organize information, set priorities, and make a small reversible decision.
Avoid: Choosing at random simply to appear decisive.

In short: A centered, gentle light lasts longer than one that dazzles.
What it means: Yellow light symbolizes reliable illumination rather than a violent flame.
Try: Show ability and care in a clear, consistent, sustainable way.
Avoid: Confusing exposure and attention with real value.

In short: Sunset is a natural stage; cherish the remaining light rather than panicking about decline.
What it means: The westering sun asks whether you can accept transition instead of turning aging into lament.
Try: Create a dignified ending and a way to pass knowledge onward.
Avoid: Denying time limits or resenting the present because it will end.

In short: Passion arriving too violently can burn brightly and then leave only ruin.
What it means: Sudden flame, death, and abandonment describe uncontrolled emotion, power, or expansion.
Try: Cool the situation, isolate danger, and wait for facts and feelings to settle.
Avoid: Treating intense emotion as an order for immediate action.

In short: Tears and concern can prompt serious protection of what matters.
What it means: Grief here is not passive pessimism but a clear recognition that something valuable could be lost.
Try: Let the emotion be acknowledged, then convert concern into prevention and care.
Avoid: Suppressing vulnerability until it erupts, or remaining in grief without action.

In short: Address the main source of harm without turning everyone associated with it into an enemy.
What it means: Capturing the leader without destroying the whole group calls for bounded, proportionate action.
Try: Focus on the harmful conduct and responsible decision-makers.
Avoid: Expanding anger into indiscriminate punishment.

gua: Hexagram 31 concerns mutual influence, like the body's natural response to a light touch. Attraction, persuasion, and cooperation are not one-way control; both sides affect each other. Feeling offers information, while the different body parts suggest different levels of maturity in the response. Identify where the impulse comes from before deciding whether to act.
lines:
In short: A feeling has only begun in the toes; it is an early signal, not a conclusion.
What it means: Attraction or concern is present but not yet strong enough to move the whole person.
Try: Observe whether it lasts and gather more interaction and facts.
Avoid: Turning a momentary thrill or worry into a major answer.

In short: Restless calves may carry you away; sitting still briefly is safer.
What it means: Anxiety, fashion, or other people's expectations are creating an urge to follow.
Try: Delay the reply or commitment until emotion subsides.
Avoid: Letting fear of missing out or disappointing someone make the decision.

In short: Blindly following another person can erase your own direction.
What it means: The thigh moves with the body; influence has become dependence rather than a chosen path.
Try: Write down your own reasons, limits, and goals before joining.
Avoid: Confusing obedience with intimacy.

In short: When your center is steady, sincere influence draws companions naturally.
What it means: Constantly calculating who will follow turns connection into anxious control.
Try: Maintain a consistent position and let genuine agreement approach freely.
Avoid: Repeated tests, strategic pleasing, and manipulation of responses.

In short: A deep response may be stable without being dramatic or easy to express.
What it means: Sensation in the back is less impulsive than hands or feet, suggesting a restrained but genuine connection.
Try: Confirm care through ordinary action and communicate it plainly.
Avoid: Denying the bond because it lacks excitement, or hiding it indefinitely.

In short: Influence made only with lips and tongue remains superficial.
What it means: Sweet speech, debate, or social messaging may be plentiful while actual responsibility is absent.
Try: Match promises with verifiable action and expect the same from others.
Avoid: Letting eloquence substitute for trust.

gua: Hexagram 32 is not rigid sameness, but a direction that can endure through change. Relationships, work, and habits require repeated investment as well as sensible adjustment. Short-lived passion cannot replace a stable rhythm, while stubbornly preserving an error is not perseverance. Ask whether the commitment can support a whole life, not merely an intense moment.
lines:
In short: Demanding depth and permanence at the very beginning makes endurance less likely.
What it means: Digging too deeply resembles asking a new relationship or system for excessive commitment immediately.
Try: Begin with a small sustainable rhythm and let trust deepen gradually.
Avoid: Using extreme vows to test loyalty or expecting instant perfection.

In short: Continued correction can gradually remove an earlier source of regret.
What it means: Improvement is not automatic; you have found a steadier position and stopped repeating the old inconsistency.
Try: Maintain what works and review it without constantly overturning it.
Avoid: Disrupting a healthy order as soon as you crave novelty.

In short: If character and promises change with convenience, respect will eventually disappear.
What it means: Today's agreement followed by tomorrow's reversal exposes a standard governed by immediate interest.
Try: Promise less, deliver what you promise, and own the gaps.
Avoid: Calling unreliability flexibility and demanding endless tolerance.

In short: Hunting forever in an empty field will not produce game.
What it means: The problem is not insufficient effort but the wrong market, audience, or method.
Try: Test whether an opportunity truly exists and change fields when needed.
Avoid: Treating time already invested as proof that the course is right.

In short: Loyalty to a role can be valuable, but fixed roles must not silence judgment.
What it means: In modern terms, steady execution may suit a supporting role, while a decision-maker who only obeys old rules neglects responsibility.
Try: Distinguish when to maintain the course and when your role requires adjustment.
Avoid: Using tradition or title to exclude suitable people from decisions.

In short: Constant change used to create a sense of activity exhausts everyone.
What it means: Repeated shaking looks energetic but gives others no direction they can rely on.
Try: Stop unnecessary revisions and execute one central goal steadily.
Avoid: Restarting everything whenever anxiety or boredom appears.

gua: Hexagram 33 is strategic withdrawal, not failure or avoidance of responsibility. When conditions are hostile, dialogue has stopped working, or boundaries are eroding, distance protects judgment and strength. A good retreat plans its timing, resources, and route rather than disappearing in emotion. Leaving an unsuitable position preserves you for more important work.
lines:
In short: You have delayed retreat and now stand at the exposed rear; this is no time to charge ahead.
What it means: As danger approaches, limited room to move makes a dramatic reversal even more hazardous.
Try: Lower your profile, protect safety, and withdraw by a clear route.
Avoid: Provoking conflict when no secure exit remains.

In short: Some commitments are bound like tough ox hide and cannot simply be discarded.
What it means: Even while withdrawing, core responsibilities, contracts, or principles still require protection.
Try: List the people, duties, and limits that cannot be abandoned, then exit the rest.
Avoid: Treating retreat as freedom from every obligation—or letting emotional pressure prevent a necessary departure.

In short: Trying to leave while attachments pull you back will eventually exhaust you.
What it means: A retreat tied by ropes resembles dependence that still needs basic care but must not control the whole direction.
Try: Complete essential handovers and define the time and scope of support.
Avoid: Delaying forever out of guilt or promising incompatible things to both sides.

In short: Being able to leave something you still enjoy is evidence of genuine choice.
What it means: A mature withdrawal recognizes long-term danger before attraction has turned into disgust or collapse.
Try: Compare the hidden cost of staying with the freedom created by leaving.
Avoid: Waiting until you are forced out.

In short: A well-timed and gracious departure can preserve both relationship and reputation.
What it means: A good retreat need not humiliate anyone or deny that worthwhile cooperation once existed.
